Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1

(1 comment)

There is a bug here, but I think the fix is smaller.

Patch Set 1:

Timothy,

Based on comments for kernel hash functions.
from linux/hash.h
>>
 * This hash multiplies the input by a large odd number and takes the
 * high bits.  Since multiplication propagates changes to the most
 * significant end only, it is essential that the high bits of the
 * product be used for the hash value.
and
/* High bits are more random, so use them. */
>>
lower bits ('&' operation) should be don't applied to the hash result and ">>" (shift) used instead.
This bug live in lustre code for long time.

Patch Set 1:

I think I see what's going on. You're fixing two bugs at the same time:

 1) Undefined behavior caused by a bug in the original patch
 2) Using high bits instead of low bits

I think that makes sense. Do you mind adding that context to the commit message?

Patch Set 1:

from my view, original patch should be use a right bits shift, so it's defect for original patch. but Ok.
Uploaded patch set 2.

parents.subject: Revert "LU-17131 ldiskfs: el9.2 encdata and filename-encode"

commit.subject: LU-17174 misc: fix hash functions
commit.message:
LU-17174 misc: fix hash functions

1) LU-16518 landing caused a bug which visible with debug kernel

UBSAN: Undefined behaviour in include/linux/hash.h:81:31
shift exponent 64 is too large for 64-bit type
	'long long unsigned int'
Call Trace:
dump_stack+0x8e/0xd0
ubsan_epilogue+0x5/0x21
ldlm_export_lock_hash+0x49/0x4d [ptlrpc]
cfs_hash_bd_from_key+0x88/0x2e0 [libcfs]

2) use a high bits unstead of low as it more accurate.

HPe-bug-id: LUS-11925
Fixes: 239e8268 (LU-16518 misc: use fixed hash code)
Signed-off-by: Alexey Lyashkov <alexey.lyashkov@hpe.com>
Change-Id: Ie1c531ad220f44e55fbf80674a49472fb6024252
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/c/fs/lustre-release/+/52611
Tested-by: jenkins <devops@whamcloud.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <maloo@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: James Simmons <jsimmons@infradead.org>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <green@whamcloud.com>
Reviewed-by: Timothy Day <timday@amazon.com>
